propose a change in the implementation of the `ReentrantLock` to improve its overall throughput for short critical sections and fix the quadratic wake-up behavior where each unlock schedules **all** waiting tasks on the lock's wait queue.

This implementation follows the same principles of the `Mutex` in the [parking_lot](https://github.com/Amanieu/parking_lot/tree/master) Rust crate which is based on the Webkit [WTF::ParkingLot](https://webkit.org/blog/6161/locking-in-webkit/) class. Only the basic working principle is implemented here, further improvements such as eventual fairness will be proposed separately.

The gist of the change is that we add one extra state to the lock, essentially going from:
```
0x0 => The lock is not locked
0x1 => The lock is locked by exactly one task. No other task is waiting for it.
0x2 => The lock is locked and some other task tried to lock but failed (conflict)
```
To:
```
# PARKED_BIT | LOCKED_BIT | Description
#     0      |     0      | The lock is not locked, nor is anyone waiting for it.
# -----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------------
#     0      |     1      | The lock is locked by exactly one task. No other task is
#            |            | waiting for it.
# -----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------------
#     1      |     0      | The lock is not locked. One or more tasks are parked.
# -----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------------
#     1      |     1      | The lock is locked by exactly one task. One or more tasks are
#            |            | parked waiting for the lock to become available.
#            |            | In this state, PARKED_BIT is only ever cleared when the cond_wait lock
#            |            | is held (i.e. on unlock). This ensures that
#            |            | we never end up in a situation where there are parked tasks but
#            |            | PARKED_BIT is not set (which would result in those tasks
#            |            | potentially never getting woken up).
```

In the current implementation we must schedule all tasks to cause a conflict (state 0x2) because on unlock we only notify any task if the lock is in the conflict state. This behavior means that with high contention and a short critical section the tasks will be effectively spinning in the scheduler queue.

With the extra state the proposed implementation has enough information to know if there are other tasks to be notified or not, which means we can always notify one task at a time while preserving the optimized path of not notifying if there are no tasks waiting. To improve throughput for short critical sections we also introduce a bounded amount of spinning before attempting to park.

### Results

Not spinning on the scheduler queue greatly reduces the CPU utilization of the following example:

```julia
function example()
    lock = ReentrantLock()
    @sync begin
        for i in 1:10000
            Threads.@spawn begin
                @lock lock begin
                    sleep(0.001)
                end
            end
        end
    end
end


@time example()
```

Current:
```
28.890623 seconds (101.65 k allocations: 7.646 MiB, 0.25% compilation time)
```
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dbd6ce57-c760-4f5a-b68a-27df6a97a46e)

Proposed:
```
22.806669 seconds (101.65 k allocations: 7.814 MiB, 0.35% compilation time)
```
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b0254180-658d-4493-86d3-dea4c500b5ac)

In a micro-benchmark where 8 threads contend for a single lock with a very short critical section we see a ~2x improvement.

Current:
```

Total iterations: 45842432
```

Total iterations: 95453184
```

In the uncontended scenario:

    x = Int[]
    as = Pair{Int, Int}[]

        end
    end

Given that this 2nd allocation will always be paired with a much bigger allocation, I don't think it matters.

Thanks for tracking it down and explaining. When you say it doesn't matter, it makes sense that it won't have a material impact in terms of working set size, but in terms of GC and the cost of scanning the live set, is this still a single GC tracked object, or is it two? If it's two then what I've experienced often with Julia is the GC quickly becoming a bottleneck in large scaled up concurrent workloads, and anything that increases the number of objects in the live set is detrimental, regardless of size.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2403154691/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

### Setup:

```julia
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> using FunctionWrappers: FunctionWrapper

julia> w = let closed_over=([1], [2])
         FunctionWrapper{typeof(closed_over), Tuple{}}(() -> closed_over)
       end;
```

### Results

Julia 1.3.1:

```julia
julia> @btime $w()
  5.435 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

Julia 1.4.2:

```julia
julia> @btime $w()
  9.919 ns (1 allocation: 16 bytes)
```

Julia 1.5.0

```julia
julia> @btime $w()
  10.112 ns (1 allocation: 32 bytes)
```

Julia 1.6.0-DEV.607

```julia
julia> @btime $w()
  11.846 ns (1 allocation: 32 bytes)
```

This turns out to be the source of a significant performance regression in Parametron.jl (which was designed to allocate zero memory).

```

My observations repeated here:

I don’t know much about hash table implementations, so maybe this is a known weakness of the algorithm chosen. I did notice `ndel` is only ever increased (in `_delete!`), then used as a condition for rehashing (if more than 3/4 slots are deleted), and then reset to 0 on rehash. Does that mean deleted slots are never re-used? I thought commonly deleted slots are treated as occupied on probe, and empty for reuse on insert.

`DataStructures.RobinDict` doesn’t exhibit the same behavior.

Code for validation:
```Julia
using BenchmarkTools
using DataStructures

function foo(d, reps)
    for i in 1:reps
        d[i] = 1
        delete!(d, i)
    end
    nothing
end

@btime foo($(Dict{Int, Int}()), 1000)
#  24.200 μs (249 allocations: 42.80 KiB)
@btime foo($(RobinDict{Int, Int}()), 1000)
#  33.101 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

I've confirmed this behavior on 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9. It's forced us to use `RobinDict` for no other reason than this allocation issue, and it's not quite as well supported and tested (we hit a nasty infinite loop bug with it today).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

My observations repeated here:

I don’t know much about hash table implementations, so maybe this is a known weakness of the algorithm chosen. I did notice `ndel` is only ever increased (in `_delete!`), then used as a condition for rehashing (if more than 3/4 slots are deleted), and then reset to 0 on rehash. Does that mean deleted slots are never re-used? I thought commonly deleted slots are treated as occupied on probe, and empty for reuse on insert.

`DataStructures.RobinDict` doesn’t exhibit the same behavior.

Code for validation:
```Julia
using BenchmarkTools
using DataStructures

function foo(d, reps)
    for i in 1:reps
        d[i] = 1
        delete!(d, i)
    end
    nothing
end

@btime foo($(Dict{Int, Int}()), 1000)
#  24.200 μs (249 allocations: 42.80 KiB)
@btime foo($(RobinDict{Int, Int}()), 1000)
#  33.101 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

I've confirmed this behavior on 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9. It's forced us to use `RobinDict` for no other reason than this allocation issue, and it's not quite as well supported and tested (we hit a nasty infinite loop bug with it today).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1341024205","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823#issuecomment-1341024205","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47823","id":1341024205,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5P7mfN","user":{"login":"ancapdev","id":2189089,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxODkwODk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2189089?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev","html_url":"https://github.com/ancapdev","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ancapdev/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-07T14:10:30Z","updated_at":"2022-12-07T14:10:30Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Excellent, thanks. * **Version**: julia-uv2-1.44.1
* **Platform**: linux

Julia version of libuv uses `vfork()` for `uv_spawn()` implementation, but contrary to `vfork()` manpage it manages file descriptors and signal handlers prior to calling `exec()`. This might work fine with vanilla kernel/libc, but breaks in our environment. We use custom code for accelerated network sockets, which breaks when the child process shares address space with the parent process.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/libuv/blob/julia-uv2-1.44.1/src/unix/process.c#L990

```
Standard description
       (From POSIX.1) The vfork() function has the same effect as
       fork(2), except that the behavior is undefined if the process
       created by vfork() either modifies any data other than a variable
       of type pid_t used to store the return value from vfork(), or
       returns from the function in which vfork() was called, or calls
       any other function before successfully calling _exit(2) or one of
       the exec(3) family of functions.

Our recommendation to vendors we've worked with on these kinds of issues is to make sure to have an API that does not rely on any interception tricks that runtime systems that manage more complicated state can opt into.

Hi @Keno. Agreed on the ideal state and what ought to be. However, vendors cater to customers, and a large fraction of customers want a drop in preload library that transparently runs their network stack faster. * **Version**: julia-uv2-1.44.1
* **Platform**: linux

Julia version of libuv uses `vfork()` for `uv_spawn()` implementation, but contrary to `vfork()` manpage it manages file descriptors and signal handlers prior to calling `exec()`. This might work fine with vanilla kernel/libc, but breaks in our environment. We use custom code for accelerated network sockets, which breaks when the child process shares address space with the parent process.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/libuv/blob/julia-uv2-1.44.1/src/unix/process.c#L990

```
Standard description
       (From POSIX.1) The vfork() function has the same effect as
       fork(2), except that the behavior is undefined if the process
       created by vfork() either modifies any data other than a variable
       of type pid_t used to store the return value from vfork(), or
       returns from the function in which vfork() was called, or calls
       any other function before successfully calling _exit(2) or one of
       the exec(3) family of functions.

function rand!(rng::Union{TaskLocalRNG, Xoshiro}, dst::Array{Float32}, ::SamplerTrivial{CloseOpen01{Float32}})
    GC.@preserve dst xoshiro_bulk(rng, convert(Ptr{UInt8}, pointer(dst)), length(dst)*4, Float32, xoshiroWidth(), _bits2float)

function rand!(rng::Union{TaskLocalRNG, Xoshiro}, dst::Array{Float64}, ::SamplerTrivial{CloseOpen01{Float64}})
    GC.@preserve dst xoshiro_bulk(rng, convert(Ptr{UInt8}, pointer(dst)), length(dst)*8, Float64, xoshiroWidth(), _bits2float)

        dst
    end
end

function rand!(rng::Union{TaskLocalRNG, Xoshiro}, dst::Array{Bool}, ::SamplerType{Bool})

Wrapping said union field in another struct, and copying that, generates optimal code.

Use case may be for instance to modify a subset of fields in an immutable struct, by constructing a new object, mostly copying existing fields. Any fields in such types which are small unions that can be trivially copied end up being an order of magnitude slower to copy than what is optimal.

Consider this example, `A` is a struct with a union field, `B` is a struct with a union field wrapped, `Option` is a simple wrapper around an optional field.
```Julia
struct Option{T}
    value::Union{Some{T}, Nothing}
end
struct A
    x::Union{Int, Nothing}
end
struct B
    x::Option{Int}
end
foo(x::T) where T = T(x.x)
```

# Codegen
All tests on Julia 1.7.2.

Codegen for construction of `A` from fields of `A` looks to be branching on the type tag and doing conditional construction. (this looks slightly better in 1.8).
```Julia
@code_native syntax=:intel debuginfo=:none foo(A(1))
```

```Asm
	cmp	byte ptr [rsi + 8], 1
	mov	rax, rdi
	jne	L32
	mov	edx, dword ptr [rsp - 7]
	mov	edi, dword ptr [rsp - 4]
	mov	rcx, qword ptr [rsi]
	mov	dword ptr [rsp - 13], edi
	mov	dword ptr [rsp - 16], edx
	mov	dl, 1
	jmp	L34
L32:
	xor	edx, edx
L34:
	mov	edi, dword ptr [rsp - 13]
	mov	esi, dword ptr [rsp - 16]
	mov	qword ptr [rax], rcx
	mov	byte ptr [rax + 8], dl
	mov	dword ptr [rsp - 21], edi
	mov	dword ptr [rsp - 24], esi
	mov	ecx, dword ptr [rsp - 24]
	mov	edx, dword ptr [rsp - 21]
	mov	dword ptr [rax + 9], ecx
	mov	dword ptr [rax + 12], edx
	ret
```

Codegen for construction of `B` from fields of `B` is as you'd hope just basic load/store operations.
```Julia
@code_native syntax=:intel debuginfo=:none foo(B(Option{Int}(Some(1))))
```
```Asm
	vmovups	xmm0, xmmword ptr [rsi]
	mov	rax, rdi
	vmovups	xmmword ptr [rdi], xmm0
	ret
```

# Timings
```Julia
@btime foo($(Ref(A(1)))[])
```
```
  12.365 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

```Julia
@btime foo($(Ref(B(Option{Int}(Some(1)))))[])
```
```
  1.303 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

# Workarounds
While the community went away from the old `Nullable{T}` to `Union{Some{T}, Nothing}`, it looks like some performance potential was left behind. Is this easy to fix and likely to resolve? If not, I'm considering a package for an `Option{T}` type to work around this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45079/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45079/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

on demand comes with some very nice theoretical benefits:
* We only optimize methods when we first call them (so we don't waste time in LLVM optimization if we never physically jump to the method in question)
* We drop the codegen lock before any LLVM optimization
    * This probably will help the most, as it decreases parallel codegen contention between multiple threads. 

But it requires some pretty high dependencies:
* JITLink must be enabled (our codegen memory manager is too restrictive for the workload of arbitrary memory allocations)
* Therefore needs LLVM14 or higher (JITLink ELF support on x86 only arrives in LLVM14)

It's also currently broken, fails with an extra symbol error during sysimage build.

I'm guessing there's a lot of implicit dependencies on other pull requests, mostly due to refactors which reduce the number of codegen global dependencies/thread safety bugs (#44573, #44454, and #44440 come to mind as a start) because of us not holding the codegen lock during optimization anymore. On 1.6 these two structs are identical in their generated code, in 1.7 `B` is boxed.
```Julia
struct A
    x::Int
end
struct B <: AbstractArray{B, 1}
    x::Int
end
```

```Julia
Base.allocatedinline(A) # true on 1.6 and 1.7
Base.allocatedinline(B) # true on 1.6, false on 1.7
@btime A($(Ref(1))[]); # 1ns and 0 allocations on 1.6 and 1.7, 
@btime B($(Ref(1))[]); # 1ns and 0 allocations on 1.6, 3.5ns and 1 allocation 1.7
```

```Julia
@code_native A(1)
```

Julia 1.6 and 1.7:
```asm
	movq	%rdi, %rax
	retq
	nopw	%cs:(%rax,%rax)
```

```Julia
@code_native B(1)
```
Julia 1.6: Same as A.
Julia 1.7:
```
	pushq	%rbx
	movq	%fs:0, %rax
	movq	-8(%rax), %rax
	movq	%rdi, %rbx
	movl	$1392, %esi                     # imm = 0x570
	movl	$16, %edx
	movq	16(%rax), %rdi
	movabsq	$140022521304849, %rax          # imm = 0x7F5988A49711
	callq	*%rax
	movabsq	$140017437570592, %rcx          # imm = 0x7F5859A0F620
	movq	%rcx, -8(%rax)
	movq	%rbx, (%rax)
	popq	%rbx
	retq
	nop
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44301/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44301/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}
